data.title: Reassessing Allegion (ALLE) After A 19% Pullback And Mixed Valuation Signals
data.description:
• Wondering if Allegion at around US$144.66 is offering fair value or a margin of safety right now? This article walks through the key valuation signals that can help you decide.
  • The stock has seen a 1.4% decline over the last 7 days and a 19.4% decline over the last 30 days, even though the 1 year return sits at 14.0% and the 3 year return at 45.5%.
  • These moves come as Allegion continues to feature in broader discussions around building products and security solutions, which keeps it on the radar of both growth focused and income focused investors. With the share price pulling back recently while longer term returns remain positive, many readers are reassessing what a reasonable valuation range might look like.
  • On Simply Wall St's valuation checks, Allegion currently holds a 5 out of 6 valuation score. The next sections break down the main valuation methods used, then finish with a way to think about valuation that goes beyond any single model.

Allegion delivered 14.0% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Building industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Allegion Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a single present value figure.

For Allegion, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow sits at about $686.7 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections in the model show annual free cash flow values between $693.2 million and $1,021.9 million over the next decade, with 2030 specifically projected at $856 million. Beyond the first five years, Simply Wall St extrapolates the figures rather than relying on additional analyst estimates.

After discounting all those projected cash flows back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $144.67 per share. With the current share price around $144.66, the implied discount is effectively 0.0%, which points to Allegion trading very close to this DCF estimate.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Allegion is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Allegion.

Approach 2: Allegion Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that business is currently generating. It helps you see how much the market is willing to pay for every dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how investors see growth and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty usually point to a lower one.

Allegion currently trades on a P/E of 19.36x. That sits just below the Building industry average of 20.21x and well below the peer group average of 41.04x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Allegion is 22.92x, which is its proprietary view of what a reasonable P/E might be after considering factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those company characteristics rather than relying on broad group averages. With Allegion’s actual P/E below the 22.92x Fair Ratio, the shares screen as undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Allegion Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, and Narratives bring this to life by letting you connect your view of Allegion’s story to a financial forecast, a fair value, and finally a comparison with the current share price. All of this is available within an easy tool on Simply Wall St’s Community page that millions of investors use to set their own assumptions for future revenue, earnings and margins, see how those inputs translate into fair values that update automatically when fresh news or earnings arrive, and compare very different viewpoints. For example, a more optimistic Allegion Narrative might be built around recurring IoT and software driven security demand that supports a fair value closer to about US$180 per share. In contrast, a more cautious Allegion Narrative might lean on lower growth, more pressure in the Americas and less confidence in acquisitions, which might leave the user comfortable with a fair value nearer to the current price. This helps each investor decide for themselves whether Allegion looks more like a buy, a hold, or a potential trim based on their own story, not just a single model output.

data.description:

Gold Resource (GORO) FY 2025 earnings snapshot

Gold Resource (GORO) closed out FY 2025 with Q4 total revenue of US$51.3 million and basic EPS of US$0.11. On a trailing 12 month basis the company remained loss making with basic EPS at a US$0.05 loss on net income of a US$6.5 million loss. Over recent periods, total revenue has ranged from US$11.2 million to US$51.3 million a quarter, while quarterly basic EPS has swung between US$0.11 and a US$0.09 loss. This gives investors a clear look at the trade off between top line momentum and ongoing earnings pressure. The mix of stronger Q4 revenue and lingering TTM losses sets up a margin focused read through for anyone weighing the durability of this earnings turn.

See our full analysis for Gold Resource.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set them against the most widely shared narratives around Gold Resource to see which stories still hold up and which need a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Q4 swings to US$18 million profit on prior losses

  • Q4 net income of US$18.0 million contrasts with a trailing 12 month net loss of US$6.5 million on US$99.8 million of revenue. The latest quarter therefore looks very different from the prior year of loss making results.
  • Bears argue that a company with a trailing 12 month loss and a five year pattern of widening losses at about 60.1% per year is still in a high risk zone, and the figures here partly back that up while also adding some tension:
    • Across FY 2025, three out of four quarters showed net losses between US$4.7 million and US$11.5 million, which aligns with the cautious view that profitability has not been consistent.
    • The sharp move from a US$14.3 million loss in Q4 FY 2024 to an US$18.0 million profit in Q4 FY 2025 gives bears fresh data to test whether this is a one off or the start of a more stable pattern.

Revenue nearly doubles quarter on quarter to US$51.3 million

  • Total revenue rose from US$24.9 million in Q3 FY 2025 to US$51.3 million in Q4. This also compares with US$12.0 million to US$13.3 million per quarter in late FY 2024, so the top line has moved around within a wide US$11.2 million to US$51.3 million range over the recent periods provided.
  • Optimistic investors often focus on revenue potential, and the forecast for revenue to grow about 12.2% per year supports that view, but the detailed numbers here give a more mixed picture:
    • Trailing 12 month revenue of US$99.8 million is higher than the US$65.7 million level a year earlier, which aligns with a growth minded thesis around the business size getting larger on this time frame.
    • However, that same trailing 12 month period still produced a US$6.5 million net loss and negative EPS of US$0.05, which keeps the bullish story tied to growth rather than to ongoing profitability.

Curious how these numbers stack up against what other investors are saying about the stock right now? 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Gold Resource.

P/S at 3.1x with ongoing losses and high volatility

  • The stock trades on a P/S of 3.1x compared with 2.5x for the US Metals & Mining industry and 3.0x for peers. At the same time, the company remained unprofitable on a trailing 12 month view and has less than one year of cash runway alongside recent shareholder dilution.
  • Critics highlight valuation and balance sheet risk, and the current data gives them several concrete reference points to work with:
    • The combination of a P/S above both industry and peer averages, plus a trailing 12 month net loss of US$6.5 million, means investors are paying more per dollar of revenue for a business that has yet to report positive earnings over that period.
    • High share price volatility over the past three months and substantial dilution in the last year tie the risk case directly to how the company is funding itself and how unpredictable shorter term returns may be around the current US$1.12 share price.

data.title: Constellation Energy Reshapes Portfolio With LS Power Sale For Calpine Deal
data.description:
• Constellation Energy (NasdaqGS:CEG) has agreed to sell a major portfolio of natural gas fired power plants to LS Power.
  • The planned divestiture is designed to satisfy antitrust conditions tied to Constellation's acquisition of Calpine.
  • This transaction represents the largest portion of regulatory required asset sales linked to the Calpine deal and is subject to approvals from the U.S. Department of Justice and FERC.

Constellation Energy operates a large U.S. power generation and energy supply business, and the Calpine acquisition is a significant step in reshaping its position in the sector. By selling a sizeable group of gas fired assets to LS Power, the company is aligning with antitrust and policy requirements while adjusting its mix of generation assets.

For you as an investor, this move is important because it affects the structure of Constellation's portfolio and how it competes in the U.S. power market. The outcome of regulatory reviews and the final scope of asset sales will influence the company's capital allocation choices and exposure to different power technologies.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Constellation Energy by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Constellation Energy.

We've flagged 3 risks for Constellation Energy. See which could impact your investment.

This asset sale sits at the intersection of Constellation Energy’s push into AI-linked nuclear power and regulators’ focus on competition in wholesale electricity markets. By agreeing to sell about 4.4 gigawatts of gas-fired capacity in the PJM region for roughly US$5b, Constellation is effectively trading some flexible thermal generation for the ability to complete the Calpine acquisition and lock in a much larger platform. That matters if you are following the company’s efforts to supply long-duration, carbon-free power to data centers run by Microsoft, Meta and other hyperscalers. A tighter focus on nuclear and geothermal could make the business more aligned with those contracts, while a smaller gas footprint may leave Constellation comparatively less exposed to merchant price swings than peers such as NextEra Energy, Duke Energy or Vistra. On the other hand, giving up dispatchable gas capacity could leave the portfolio more reliant on the nuclear fleet for reliability and on Calpine’s remaining assets for flexibility, which is worth keeping in mind as electricity demand tied to AI infrastructure grows.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Constellation Energy Narrative

  • The divestiture helps clear a key regulatory hurdle for the Calpine acquisition, supporting the narrative that Constellation can expand capacity and product offerings to serve long-term, data center focused contracts.
  • Shifting a portion of gas-fired assets out of the portfolio could challenge the idea that Constellation will rely on a broad mix of nuclear and gas to manage rising AI data center demand if future reliability depends more heavily on the remaining fleet.
  • The specific loss of 4.4 gigawatts of PJM gas capacity and the US$5b transaction value are not directly reflected in the narrative, which may mean investors using that story should reassess assumptions about future fuel mix and cash flow resilience.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Constellation Energy to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentration risk could rise if Constellation leans even more on its nuclear fleet and a smaller set of gas plants to meet high, data center driven demand.
  • ⚠️ The transaction still depends on approvals from the U.S. Department of Justice and FERC, so delays, additional conditions or a need for further asset sales would add execution risk.
  • 🎁 Completing this sale is a key step toward fully closing out antitrust commitments, which can reduce regulatory overhang from the Calpine deal.
  • 🎁 Recycling US$5b from gas assets may give Constellation more flexibility to fund nuclear uprates, restarts and long-term infrastructure serving AI-related customers.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on three things. First, whether the DOJ and FERC sign off on this transaction on the expected timetable and whether any extra remedies are requested. Second, how Constellation redeploys the US$5b in proceeds, especially across nuclear uprates, potential share repurchases or further energy infrastructure projects. Third, how the combined Constellation and Calpine portfolio looks once all required sales are complete, including the balance between nuclear, gas and geothermal capacity and how that compares with peers that are also chasing AI-related electricity demand. Those pieces will shape how durable the company’s cash flows look over time.

In this case, management has framed Nitesh Sharan’s departure as personal and unrelated to accounting or policy disagreements, which helps reduce the risk of this being read as a red flag on financial reporting.   • 🎁 The company’s recent partnerships in areas like healthcare and retail, plus traction with agentic AI in vehicles alongside large players such as Alphabet, Amazon, and Microsoft, give the incoming permanent CFO a growing commercial base to work with.

Approach 1: Lockheed Martin Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today, using a required rate of return.

For Lockheed Martin, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $6.8b. Analyst based estimates and subsequent extrapolations suggest annual free cash flow projections around $6.7b in 2026 and $7.6b by 2030, with later years based on Simply Wall St estimates rather than direct analyst forecasts.

Investors often look for a P/E that lines up with their view of the company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can sometimes support a higher “normal” P/E, while slower growth or higher risk can point to a lower one.

Lockheed Martin currently trades on a P/E of 29.46x. data.title: PepsiCo Water Gains And Protein Snacks Reshape Growth And Risk Story
data.description:
• PepsiCo (NasdaqGS:PEP) reports it has met its 2025 pep+ water stewardship goals ahead of schedule, replenishing all water used at high risk sites.
  • The company has adopted global water stewardship standards across its facilities as part of its broader pep+ sustainability agenda.
  • PepsiCo has launched Good Warrior, a protein based meat stick brand, entering the protein snacks category.
  • The combination of early ESG goal completion and a new health focused product line could be relevant for how investors view PepsiCo’s risk profile and growth options.

PepsiCo operates a large global snacks and beverages business, so water use and resource efficiency are central to its long term operations. Meeting its pep+ water goals ahead of the 2025 target adds a concrete ESG data point that investors can weigh alongside more traditional financial metrics. At the same time, the move into protein snacks with Good Warrior shows PepsiCo responding to consumer interest in higher protein, convenience focused options.

For shareholders in NasdaqGS:PEP, these updates sit alongside the usual questions around margins, pricing power, and capital allocation. Over time, progress on water stewardship and expansion into protein based snacks could influence how resilient PepsiCo’s brand portfolio and supply chain appear, and how the company is positioned if health and sustainability keep shaping consumer spending.

For you as an investor, PepsiCo’s early completion of its pep+ water goals and the Good Warrior launch both speak directly to how the business model could evolve. Water stewardship targets sit close to core manufacturing risk, especially for a company that relies heavily on water-intensive beverages and snacks. Meeting those goals ahead of schedule may help reduce long-term operational and regulatory risk, particularly in high water-risk regions where supply interruptions or compliance costs can be material. At the same time, Good Warrior moves PepsiCo into protein-based snacks, a category that taps health-conscious, higher-protein demand already targeted by peers such as Mondelez and Nestlé. The key question is execution, including how well PepsiCo can scale distribution, secure quality protein sourcing, and avoid cannibalizing existing snacks while protecting margins. Together with other moves like reformulating legacy brands and acquiring better-for-you offerings, this news gives you more detail on how PepsiCo is trying to balance sustainability, product reformulation, and category expansion against cost pressures and brand complexity.

How This Fits Into The PepsiCo Narrative

  • The early water stewardship achievements support the narrative catalyst around sustainability and operational efficiency, which aims to make PepsiCo’s supply chain more resilient and cost-efficient over time.
  • The push into meat-based protein snacks could challenge the narrative if execution risks or portfolio complexity offset the benefits from health-oriented products and productivity programs.
  • The specific focus on water-risk mitigation and protein snacks may not be fully captured in the existing narrative, which centers more broadly on health-focused reformulation, international expansion, and technology-driven efficiency.

It is also useful to watch how management balances spending on ESG projects and health-oriented launches with the already highlighted need to keep dividends and other cash uses on a sustainable footing. All of this will sit alongside upcoming earnings, where commentary on the pep+ program and the early performance of newer products can help you judge whether these initiatives are gaining traction.
